proposals.title: Eye-Tracking Reveals the Temporal Dynamics of Sublexical Rhythm and Phoneme Processing During Nonsense Word Reading in Children At-Risk for Dyslexia
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 research by Goswami and others has established that impaired sensitivity to speech rhythm (prosody) and phonemic timing cues (e.g., voice onset time) is a core deficit in developmental dyslexia, potentially disrupting the formation of robust phonological representations. However, the precise temporal dynamics of how these rhythmic and sublexical units are processed during the act of reading itself remain opaque. Eye-tracking provides a real-time, implicit measure of cognitive processing that can reveal these dynamics.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that children at risk for dyslexia will show atypical eye-movement patterns (e.g., increased fixations, more regressions) specifically on rhythmic units (syllables, stress patterns) and phonemic boundaries within nonsense words, and that these patterns will correlate with independent measures of phonological and rhythmic sensitivity.
proposals.method: We will use a high-speed remote eye-tracker (e.g., Tobii Pro Spectrum) to record fixation durations, saccade lengths, and regressions as children (aged 5-6 years, at-risk and typically developing) read aloud visually presented nonsense words. Stimuli will be systematically manipulated for rhythmic structure (e.g., trochaic vs. iambic stress) and phonemic difficulty (e.g., within- vs. across-category VOT contrasts). Data will be processed using standard algorithms (e.g., I-VT filter for fixation identification) and analyzed using linear mixed-effects models to compare groups and conditions across time bins aligned to word presentation.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first fine-grained, millisecond-level account of how rhythmic and phonemic information guides sublexical decoding in real time, directly linking Goswami's temporal processing framework to the oculomotor mechanics of reading. It will identify specific processing stages where the at-risk trajectory diverges, offering precise targets for rhythm-based interventions aimed at stabilizing phonological assembly.
proposals.novelty: While eye-tracking has been used to study lexical reading, its application to *nonsense word* reading to isolate sublexical processing of *speech rhythm cues* is novel. This directly tests the hypothesis that the dyslexia deficit is in temporal sampling of the speech signal, not just lexical access.

proposals.title: Neural Priming for Orthography and Phonology in Nonsense Words: An EEG Study of Children with and without Dyslexia
proposals.motivation: Developmental dyslexia is strongly linked to phonological processing deficits, but the role of orthographic processing and its neural interaction with phonology during early reading acquisition is less clear. Pre-2026 research by Goswami and others highlights the importance of temporal (rhythmic) processing and neural sensitivity to speech sound contrasts in dyslexia, yet the real-time neural dynamics of orthographic-phonological integration during novel word processing remain underexplored.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that children with dyslexia will show reduced and/or delayed neural priming effects, particularly for phonological primes, during nonsense word processing compared to typically developing children, reflecting a core deficit in the automatic integration of orthographic and phonological representations.
proposals.method: We will employ high-density EEG to measure event-related potentials (ERPs) during a masked priming lexical decision task. Children (aged 7-9 years) will process visually presented nonsense word targets (e.g., 'bink') preceded by briefly presented, masked primes that are orthographically similar (e.g., 'bink' -> 'bink'), phonologically similar (e.g., 'bink' -> 'binc'), or unrelated. We will analyze established ERP components linked to orthographic (N170/N1) and phonological (N400) processing, using time-frequency analysis to assess oscillatory dynamics (e.g., theta and gamma band activity) associated with priming.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide a direct neural signature of orthographic and phonological priming during the critical stage of learning to read. It will clarify whether dyslexia involves a specific deficit in phonological priming, a general deficit in cross-code integration, or atypical oscillatory entrainment during priming. The findings will inform theories of reading development and could identify novel neural targets for rhythm-based interventions aimed at improving phonological sensitivity.
proposals.novelty: While priming studies exist, this proposal uniquely combines masked priming of nonsense words with high-temporal-resolution EEG in children at risk, allowing us to dissociate the neural timecourse of orthographic vs. phonological activation pre-lexically, a key innovation over behavioral or fMRI studies.

proposals.title: Rhythmic Phonological Templates in Early Literacy: The Role of Rhyme Pattern Frequency in Phonological Processing and Dyslexia Risk
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 research by Goswami and others has established that sensitivity to speech rhythm, particularly amplitude envelope rise time, is a core deficit in developmental dyslexia and a precursor to phonological development. However, the role of higher-order rhythmic phonological structures, specifically the statistical frequency of rhyme patterns (e.g., -at, -og) in the lexicon, in shaping children's phonological templates and their link to literacy acquisition is poorly understood. This gap limits the precision of early identification and intervention strategies.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that children's sensitivity to and learning of novel phonological forms is modulated by the frequency of their constituent rhyme patterns in the ambient language, and that this statistical learning mechanism is impaired in children at familial risk for dyslexia, contributing to their phonological deficits.
proposals.method: We will design a set of auditory non-word learning tasks where the rhyme patterns (e.g., /-ɪp/, /-ʌg/) are systematically varied in their frequency based on pre-2026 child-directed speech corpora (e.g., CHILDES). Using EEG, we will measure neural sensitivity (MMN and P3 components) to these patterns in 4- to 5-year-old children at high and low familial risk for dyslexia. Behavioral tasks will assess immediate recognition and subsequent recall of the non-words. Analysis will employ linear mixed-effects models to dissociate the effects of rhyme frequency, risk status, and their interaction on neural and behavioral metrics.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first direct evidence for how the statistical structure of the phonological lexicon scaffolds early phonological learning, moving beyond simple acoustic processing. It will identify a specific, quantifiable deficit in statistical phonological learning in at-risk children, offering a novel biomarker for dyslexia risk. The findings will directly inform the design of rhythm-based interventions by specifying which phonological patterns to target for maximal therapeutic gain.
proposals.novelty: While prior work has examined sensitivity to acoustic cues like voice onset time, this proposal uniquely investigates sensitivity to the statistical frequency of supra-segmental phonological *patterns*, bridging Goswami's rhythm framework with statistical learning theories of language acquisition. The explicit manipulation of rhyme pattern frequency as an independent variable in a neural learning paradigm with at-risk preschoolers is a novel approach.

proposals.title: Rhythmic Entrainment of Attention and Memory Networks: A Neuroimaging-Informed Intervention for At-Risk Preschoolers
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 research shows that children at risk for dyslexia exhibit atypical neural processing of temporal speech cues like voice onset time (VOT) and have deficits in rhythm perception, which is linked to phonological development. Concurrent neuroimaging evidence indicates that attention and memory networks (e.g., fronto-parietal and hippocampal systems) are crucial for language learning. However, instructional design rarely leverages these specific neural mechanisms to structure learning.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a rhythm-based training program, explicitly designed to entrain neural oscillations in attention and memory networks, will enhance neural sensitivity to phonological contrasts and improve pre-literacy skills in 4- to 5-year-olds at familial risk for developmental dyslexia.
proposals.method: We will design a tablet-based intervention using rhythmic auditory stimulation (e.g., drumming, metrical speech games) with varying tempos and complexity to target attentional orienting (phasic alertness) and working memory updating. Using a pre-post design, we will measure outcomes with (1) EEG to assess neural sensitivity to within- and across-category VOT contrasts via mismatch negativity (MMN) and time-frequency analysis of theta/gamma oscillations, and (2) behavioral tests of phonological awareness, rhythm perception, and executive function. Data will be analyzed using mixed-effects models and connectivity analyses (e.g., phase-locking value) to link intervention-induced neural changes to behavioral gains.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide a direct, mechanistic test of how rhythm training influences the neural substrates of speech processing. It will yield a principled, neurobiologically-grounded framework for designing early interventions, moving beyond behavioral correlations to target specific cognitive networks. The findings will inform theories of the rhythm-language link by elucidating the mediating role of attention and memory.
proposals.novelty: While rhythm training has been explored, this proposal uniquely designs the intervention parameters (e.g., tempo, predictability) based on pre-2026 neuroimaging models of attentional phasing and memory consolidation, explicitly aiming to drive plasticity in those systems. It directly tests the causal pathway from rhythmic entrainment to phonological neural sensitivity, which is non-obvious given that prior work primarily shows correlation.

proposals.title: Longitudinal trajectories of neural rhythm sensitivity and their predictive relationship to reading acquisition and dyslexia risk
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 research by Goswami and others has established that children's sensitivity to speech rhythm (e.g., amplitude envelope rise time) is a key predictor of phonological development and dyslexia risk. However, the neurodevelopmental trajectory of this sensitivity and its causal-temporal links to later reading outcomes remain poorly understood due to a lack of longitudinal, multi-method studies tracking the same children from pre-reading to literacy stages.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the developmental trajectory of neural sensitivity to rhythmic speech cues (measured via EEG) between ages 3 and 7 years will predict the trajectory of phonological skill development, which in turn will mediate the relationship to reading achievement at age 8, with flatter neural sensitivity growth curves characterizing children who develop dyslexia.
proposals.method: We will conduct a 5-year longitudinal cohort study of 200 children (100 with familial risk for dyslexia). Annual assessments at ages 3, 4, 5, 6, and 7 will include: (1) EEG recording during passive listening to within- and across-category voice onset time (VOT) contrasts and amplitude-modulated rhythmic stimuli to derive neural indices of temporal and phonological processing; (2) behavioral tests of rhythm perception, phonological awareness, and executive function; (3) standardized reading and spelling assessments at school age. Data will be analyzed using latent growth curve modeling and cross-lagged panel models to test directional predictions and mediation.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first detailed map of the co-development of neural rhythm processing and reading-related skills, identifying critical sensitive periods and neural benchmarks. It will clarify whether rhythmic deficits are a primary cause or a secondary correlate of phonological deficits in dyslexia, directly informing the theoretical basis for rhythm-based interventions.
proposals.novelty: While prior work has shown cross-sectional correlations, this is the first proposal to model the *dynamic, longitudinal interplay* between neural rhythm sensitivity, phonological skill, and reading in the same children from pre-reading through literacy acquisition, using a design that can test causal pathways.

proposals.title: Neural Coupling of Rhythmic Speech Entrainment During Teacher-Child Reading Instruction and its Relation to Phonological Sensitivity
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 research shows that children's neural sensitivity to speech rhythm is foundational for phonological development and literacy, and deficits in this sensitivity are linked to dyslexia. However, the role of the teacher's brain in shaping this sensitivity during live, naturalistic instruction is unknown. Understanding the teacher-student neural interaction is crucial for developing effective, neuroscience-informed literacy interventions.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the degree of neural coupling between a teacher's and a child's low-frequency oscillatory activity during rhythmic speech instruction (e.g., reading nursery rhymes) will predict the child's subsequent gains in phonological sensitivity, and that this coupling will be weaker for children at risk for dyslexia.
proposals.method: We will use dual-EEG hyperscanning to record brain activity from teacher-child dyads during a controlled reading-aloud task. We will analyze neural coupling by computing phase-locking values (PLVs) and Granger causality in the theta (4-8 Hz) and delta (1-4 Hz) bands, known for tracking speech rhythm. Pre- and post-session assessments will measure children's phonological sensitivity using standardized tests (e.g., rhyme oddity) and voice onset time (VOT) discrimination tasks. We will compare coupling metrics between typically developing children and those at familial risk for dyslexia.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first direct evidence of how teacher-student neural synchrony during rhythm-based instruction supports phonological learning. It will identify a potential neural mechanism underlying effective pedagogical rhythm and offer objective biomarkers for evaluating the efficacy of rhythm-based interventions for dyslexia.
proposals.novelty: While prior work has examined child neural processing or teacher behavior separately, this proposal directly tests the *interactive neural dyad* as the unit of analysis for literacy instruction. It applies hyperscanning, previously used in social cognition, to the specific domain of rhythmic phonological teaching, a core interest of Goswami's pre-2026 research.

proposals.title: Causal Primacy of Amplitude Envelope Processing in Developmental Dyslexia: A Longitudinal Intervention and Neural Entrainment Study
proposals.motivation: The rhythmic amplitude envelope (AE) of speech is crucial for phonological segmentation and reading acquisition. Pre-2026 theories of dyslexia debate whether impaired AE processing is a core causal deficit or a secondary consequence of broader auditory or attentional problems. Resolving this causality is critical for designing effective early interventions targeting the correct perceptual mechanism.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a deficit in neural entrainment to slow amplitude modulation rates (<10 Hz) is a primary, causal factor in developmental dyslexia, preceding and predicting phonological and reading deficits, and that targeted AE training will yield greater improvements in reading-related skills than control interventions addressing general auditory or attentional abilities.
proposals.method: We will conduct a longitudinal intervention study with 5- to 6-year-old children at familial risk for dyslexia. Children will be randomly assigned to one of three 8-week training programs: (1) AE-based rhythm training using amplitude-modulated non-speech and speech stimuli, (2) a control auditory frequency discrimination training, or (3) a control visual attention training. Pre-, post-, and 6-month follow-up assessments will include EEG to measure neural entrainment to amplitude-modulated sounds, standardized tests of phonological awareness, and reading precursors. We will use time-frequency analysis (Morlet wavelets) and phase-locking value (PLV) to quantify neural entrainment, and hierarchical linear modeling to analyze growth trajectories.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first direct causal test of the AE deficit hypothesis against competing accounts in a pre-reading at-risk sample. It will clarify the mechanistic pathway from low-level auditory rhythm processing to literacy, informing theoretical models of dyslexia. The results will directly guide the development of more precise, neuroscience-informed intervention protocols.
proposals.novelty: The novel integration of a longitudinal intervention design with direct neural measures of entrainment allows for testing causal primacy, moving beyond the correlational evidence dominant in pre-2026 literature. The direct comparison of AE training against active control interventions targeting alternative deficits is a critical and previously underutilized approach.

proposals.title: Amplitude Envelope Processing as a Core Deficit or a Subtype Marker: A Multi-Modal Investigation of Developmental Dyslexia
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 research, including work by Goswami, suggests that impaired sensitivity to the amplitude envelope (AE) of speech is a key deficit in developmental dyslexia, linked to phonological and rhythmic processing difficulties. However, it is unclear whether this deficit is a universal 'core' feature present in all individuals with dyslexia or characterizes a specific neurocognitive subtype. Resolving this is critical for developing targeted, theoretically grounded interventions.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that deficits in amplitude envelope sensitivity will not be uniform across all individuals with dyslexia. Instead, they will define a specific subtype characterized by pronounced rhythmic and prosodic processing difficulties, distinct from other potential dyslexic profiles.
proposals.method: We will recruit 120 children (8-10 years), including 80 with a dyslexia diagnosis and 40 typically developing controls. Using EEG, we will measure neural entrainment to amplitude-modulated tones and natural speech, focusing on low-frequency (<10 Hz) phase-locking. Behavioral tasks will assess sensitivity to amplitude rise time and rhythm perception. We will apply hierarchical cluster analysis to the combined neural and behavioral data from the dyslexic group to identify potential subtypes, followed by discriminant function analysis to validate cluster separation.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first direct test of whether amplitude envelope deficits are a universal or subtype-specific feature of dyslexia. It will advance the theoretical debate on the causal basis of dyslexia and offer an empirical foundation for moving towards personalized, rhythm-based interventions tailored to an individual's neurocognitive profile.
proposals.novelty: While AE deficits are well-documented, their universality is assumed rather than tested. This proposal uniquely combines neural entrainment and behavioral measures with data-driven subtyping to formally challenge the 'uniform deficit' model, a non-obvious approach given the field's historical focus on group averages.

proposals.title: Targeted Amplitude Envelope Onset Training (TAEOT): A Randomized Controlled Trial to Improve Phonological Processing in Children at Familial Risk for Dyslexia
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 research, including work by Goswami, strongly implicates impaired neural sensitivity to the amplitude envelope's rise time (onset) as a core auditory deficit in developmental dyslexia, potentially disrupting phonological development. Existing auditory training programs are often broad, lacking specificity for this precise temporal cue. A targeted intervention could provide a more direct test of the causal role of envelope perception and offer a novel, theoretically grounded remediation pathway.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a computerized auditory training program specifically designed to sharpen perception of amplitude envelope onsets will lead to significant improvements in both low-level envelope processing and higher-order phonological awareness in 4- to 5-year-old children at familial risk for dyslexia, compared to an active control group.
proposals.method: We will design a gamified, adaptive training program using MATLAB/Psychtoolbox. The core task will require children to discriminate or detect subtle differences in the rise times of amplitude-modulated sounds (e.g., 'ba' vs. 'wa'). The algorithm will adapt trial difficulty in real-time using a 2-down-1-up staircase procedure, maintaining ~71% accuracy. We will conduct a double-blind RCT with 60 at-risk children, randomly assigned to 6 weeks of TAEOT or a control training targeting spectral discrimination. Pre-, post-, and 3-month follow-up assessments will include EEG (measuring neural sensitivity to within- and across-category VOT and envelope contrasts), standardized tests of phonological awareness, and rhythm processing tasks.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide a direct causal test of the amplitude envelope rise time hypothesis of dyslexia. It will determine whether targeted remediation of this specific auditory temporal deficit can cascade to improvements in phonological skills, a key predictor of reading success. The findings will inform the development of more precise, mechanism-based interventions for language and literacy disorders.
proposals.novelty: While rhythm-based interventions exist, this proposal uniquely focuses on the precise acoustic parameter (envelope onset) theorized to be the foundational deficit, moving from correlation to causation via targeted training. It integrates Goswami's pre-2026 findings on neural sensitivity to temporal cues with a novel, adaptive training architecture to directly manipulate the proposed core impairment.

proposals.title: Mapping the Perceptual Weighting of Amplitude Envelope Onsets in Children with and without Dyslexia using Psychophysical Reverse Correlation
proposals.motivation: The amplitude envelope's rhythmic structure is crucial for speech segmentation and phonological development. Impaired sensitivity to envelope onsets is a core deficit in developmental dyslexia, but the precise acoustic features (e.g., rise time, sharpness, spectral composition) that children perceptually weight remain poorly specified. A fine-grained, data-driven map of these perceptual cues is needed to refine auditory processing models of dyslexia and inform targeted interventions.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that children with dyslexia will show a noisier and less consistent perceptual weighting profile for envelope onset features compared to typically developing children, with a specific deficit in weighting rapid, low-frequency modulated onsets critical for speech rhythm.
proposals.method: We will adapt a two-interval forced-choice psychophysical reverse correlation paradigm. On each trial, children will hear two noise-vocoded speech-like stimuli with complex, randomly modulated amplitude envelopes and identify which has a clearer 'beat' (onset). The envelope modulations will be generated by summing multiple sinusoids with random phases and amplitudes. The classification image—the average envelope difference between 'beat-present' and 'beat-absent' choices—will be computed via linear regression, revealing the time-frequency features driving perception. We will compare these perceptual kernels between 6-7-year-old children at familial risk for dyslexia and age-matched controls.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first quantitative, feature-based 'perceptual fingerprint' of envelope onset processing in young children, directly linking specific acoustic deficits to dyslexia risk. It will advance theories of rhythmic processing by moving beyond threshold measures to model internal perceptual templates. The findings will offer precise targets for auditory training programs aiming to sharpen onset sensitivity.
proposals.novelty: While reverse correlation is established in vision and adult speech perception, its application to map the perceptual weighting of complex rhythmic features in young children, especially those with dyslexia, is novel. This moves beyond correlational links between rhythm and language to a causal perceptual modeling approach.

proposals.title: Pre-attentive Processing of Amplitude Envelope Rise Time in Children with and without Dyslexia: An EEG Mismatch Negativity Study
proposals.motivation: The amplitude envelope, particularly the rate of amplitude rise time, is a crucial acoustic cue for speech rhythm and syllable segmentation. Pre-2026 research by Goswami and others has established that children with developmental dyslexia show behavioural deficits in processing rise time. However, it remains unclear whether this deficit originates in pre-attentive, low-level cortical auditory processing or later, attention-dependent stages. Establishing this neural locus is critical for refining causal models of dyslexia and designing targeted interventions.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that children with dyslexia will show a significantly reduced or delayed mismatch negativity (MMN) response to subtle changes in amplitude envelope rise time compared to typically developing children, indicating a pre-attentive cortical processing deficit for this fundamental rhythmic cue.
proposals.method: We will employ a classic auditory oddball EEG paradigm. Standard stimuli will be amplitude-modulated tones or non-speech sounds with a specific rise time. Deviant stimuli will differ only in their rate of amplitude rise. EEG will be recorded from 64 channels while children watch a silent film. The MMN will be derived by subtracting the ERP to the standard from the ERP to the deviant. Analysis will focus on MMN amplitude, latency, and scalp topography over temporal and frontal regions. We will recruit 30 children with dyslexia and 30 age-matched controls (8-10 years).
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first direct neural evidence for a pre-attentive cortical deficit in amplitude envelope processing in dyslexia, linking low-level auditory neuroscience directly to the phonological deficit hypothesis. It will clarify the stage of processing affected, informing whether interventions should target basic auditory encoding or higher-level phonological integration. The findings will also contribute to the debate on the neural basis of rhythm sensitivity in language development.
proposals.novelty: While MMN has been used to study phoneme processing (e.g., voice onset time) in dyslexia, its application to the specific, non-phonemic acoustic cue of amplitude envelope rise time is novel. This approach directly tests the 'rise time sensitivity hypothesis' of dyslexia at the pre-attentive neural level, a key gap in pre-2026 literature.

proposals.title: Biophysical modelling of auditory neuron responses to amplitude envelope onsets: A computational mechanism for impaired rhythmic perception in developmental dyslexia
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 research by Goswami and others strongly links impaired sensitivity to speech rhythm (amplitude envelope onsets) to developmental dyslexia, suggesting a core auditory processing deficit. However, the precise neural mechanisms underlying this impaired 'rise time' sensitivity remain unclear, limiting targeted interventions. Bridging the gap between behavioural findings and neural function is a critical next step for the field.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that impaired perceptual sensitivity to amplitude envelope rise times in dyslexia can be simulated by altering specific biophysical properties (e.g., potassium channel kinetics) in single-neuron models of auditory neurons, leading to degraded temporal encoding of rhythmic patterns.
proposals.method: We will develop Hodgkin-Huxley-style compartmental models of auditory midbrain neurons (e.g., inferior colliculus) using the NEURON simulation environment. Models will be driven by auditory stimuli with systematically varied amplitude envelope rise times. We will simulate the proposed deficit by manipulating parameters controlling post-inhibitory rebound and adaptation dynamics. Model output (spike timing precision) will be compared to existing psychoacoustic and electrophysiological data from typically developing children and those at risk for dyslexia.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first explicit, biophysically grounded computational model of the proposed auditory deficit in dyslexia, moving beyond correlational evidence. It will generate testable predictions for future in vivo electrophysiology and identify potential neurophysiological targets for intervention (e.g., via auditory training or neuromodulation).
proposals.novelty: While computational models of dyslexia exist, they are typically abstract or network-level; this proposal's novelty lies in scaling down to the biophysical properties of single auditory neurons to directly model the perceptual deficit in envelope processing.

proposals.title: The Upper Limits of Amplitude Envelope Perception: A Comparative Study of Superior Readers and Musicians
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 research strongly links sensitivity to the amplitude envelope (the slow rhythmic modulations in speech) to phonological development and reading acquisition, with deficits observed in dyslexia. However, the field has focused primarily on deficits and typical development, leaving the upper limits of this perceptual skill unexplored. Understanding the exceptional performance of superior readers and musicians can reveal the full potential of the auditory system for language processing and inform the design of more effective, high-ceiling interventions.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that superior readers and musicians will demonstrate significantly enhanced sensitivity to fine-grained amplitude envelope contrasts compared to typical readers, and that their performance will correlate with advanced phonological processing and working memory skills.
proposals.method: We will recruit three age-matched groups (9-11 years): superior readers (top 10% on standardized tests), child musicians (>3 years training), and typical readers. Using a psychoacoustic paradigm, we will measure thresholds for detecting subtle modulations in the amplitude envelope of noise carriers and speech-like stimuli. Neural sensitivity will be assessed using EEG, focusing on the mismatch negativity (MMN) component to within- and across-category envelope contrasts. All participants will complete standardized tests of phonological awareness, rapid naming, and executive function.
proposals.contribution: This study will establish the upper bounds of envelope perception in development, directly linking exceptional auditory rhythm processing to exceptional reading. It will provide a neural and behavioral benchmark for 'optimal' auditory processing, offering new targets for intervention. The comparison between readers and musicians will clarify whether enhanced envelope perception is domain-general or reading-specific.
proposals.novelty: While prior work examines deficits and norms, this proposal uniquely investigates the superior performance end of the spectrum. It directly tests whether the 'rhythm advantage' observed in musicians extends to an analogous, language-specific skill in superior readers, moving beyond correlation to characterize the mechanisms of expertise.

proposals.title: Beyond Rise Time: The Relative Contribution of Amplitude Envelope Decay and Modulation to Meter Perception in Children with and without Dyslexia
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 research, including seminal work by Goswami, established the critical role of amplitude envelope rise time in speech rhythm processing and its impairment in developmental dyslexia. However, the amplitude envelope is a multi-feature signal. To develop more precise models of rhythm perception and its deficits, it is essential to systematically test the perceptual weight of other envelope features, such as decay time and modulation depth, in the foundational skill of meter perception.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that amplitude envelope decay time and modulation depth are significant, independent predictors of meter perception accuracy in children, and that their relative importance differs between children with and without dyslexia, potentially revealing a broader envelope processing deficit.
proposals.method: We will design non-linguistic rhythmic sequences where rise time, decay time, and modulation depth are orthogonally and parametrically manipulated using digital signal processing (e.g., in MATLAB or Python). Child-friendly behavioral tasks (e.g., tapping to the beat, same/different meter judgments) will assess meter perception. We will employ hierarchical linear regression and machine learning models (e.g., Random Forest) to quantify the unique variance in performance explained by each envelope feature. Neural sensitivity will be measured using EEG, focusing on mismatch negativity (MMN) and steady-state responses to the different envelope contrasts.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first systematic decomposition of the amplitude envelope's role in meter perception, moving beyond the dominant focus on rise time. It will generate a more nuanced model of rhythm processing deficits in dyslexia, which could inform the design of more targeted auditory training interventions that manipulate a wider set of acoustic cues.
proposals.novelty: While prior work identifies rise time as a key deficit, it is non-obvious whether other envelope features are merely correlated or are independently causal for rhythm perception. This proposal explicitly tests their independent perceptual weighting and neural encoding, a crucial step for mechanistic theory.

proposals.title: Shared Neural Oscillatory Mechanisms for Meter, Amplitude Envelope, and Phonological Processing in Children with and without Dyslexia
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 research, particularly by Goswami, establishes that sensitivity to amplitude envelope rise time and rhythmic timing is a core deficit in developmental dyslexia, linked to phonological difficulties. However, the neural mechanisms linking the perception of musical meter (a higher-order rhythmic structure) to rise time and phonological processing remain unclear. Identifying shared brain mechanisms is crucial for understanding the auditory temporal processing deficits underlying dyslexia and informing rhythmic interventions.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that neural entrainment to meter and amplitude envelope rise time in auditory cortex and the basal ganglia will be correlated, and that the strength of this entrainment will predict phonological processing skills. We further hypothesize that children with dyslexia will show reduced neural entrainment to both meter and rise time compared to typically developing controls.
proposals.method: We will use magnetoencephalography (MEG) to measure neural oscillations in 7- to 9-year-old children (n=60, 30 with dyslexia). Stimuli will include rhythmic tone sequences with varying metrical structures and amplitude-modulated noise with varying rise times. We will analyze neural entrainment using time-frequency decomposition (Morlet wavelets) and phase-locking value (PLV) to quantify synchronization to the stimulus rhythms. Source localization (beamforming) will identify key regions (e.g., auditory cortex, basal ganglia). Correlations between neural entrainment metrics and behavioral measures of phonological awareness and reading will be computed.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first direct neurophysiological evidence for shared oscillatory mechanisms underlying meter and rise time perception, directly testing the 'temporal sampling' framework of dyslexia. It will clarify whether deficits in meter processing are a primary neural signature of dyslexia, offering a more precise target for rhythm-based interventions aimed at improving phonological skills.
proposals.novelty: While prior work links behavioral rise time sensitivity to dyslexia, this proposal uniquely uses MEG to directly compare neural entrainment to meter and rise time within the same children, testing their convergence as a neural predictor of phonological ability. This moves beyond behavioral correlations to identify a common neural oscillatory deficit.

proposals.title: Precise Psychophysical Measurement of Amplitude Envelope Rise Time Sensitivity in Children: A Cross-Species Paradigm for Dyslexia Risk
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 research by Goswami and others has established that impaired sensitivity to amplitude envelope rise time—a key temporal cue for speech rhythm—is a core auditory deficit in developmental dyslexia. However, human testing paradigms often lack the precision and parametric control of animal auditory psychophysics, limiting our ability to isolate the specific neural mechanisms underlying this deficit. Adapting these rigorous methods for children is crucial to move from correlation to a mechanistic understanding of the rise time processing impairment.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that children at familial risk for dyslexia will show significantly elevated psychophysical thresholds and slower neural entrainment to amplitude-modulated sounds with systematically varied rise times compared to typically developing controls, specifically for rise times in the behaviorally relevant 15-300ms range critical for speech syllable parsing.
proposals.method: We will adapt a two-alternative forced-choice (2AFC) 'go/no-go' paradigm, standard in rodent auditory research, for children aged 4-6 years. Stimuli will be amplitude-modulated broadband noise or tones where rise time is the sole, parametrically varied feature (e.g., 10-400ms linear vs. nonlinear ramps). Thresholds will be determined using a transformed up-down staircase procedure (e.g., Levitt's method). Concurrently, we will use high-density EEG to measure cortical entrainment (phase-locking value) to the modulation rate, linking perceptual thresholds to neural oscillatory precision.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first high-resolution psychophysical function for rise time processing in young children at risk for dyslexia, establishing a causal model linking specific auditory temporal deficits to phonological development. It will create a gold-standard, translatable paradigm for early identification and for evaluating the efficacy of rhythm-based interventions by offering precise, mechanism-driven biomarkers.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the direct cross-species translation of a controlled animal psychophysics paradigm to human developmental research, allowing for unprecedented isolation of the rise time parameter, which is typically confounded with other acoustic features in standard human auditory processing tests.

proposals.title: White Matter Integrity in Auditory-Motor Pathways Predicts Neural Sensitivity to Rhythmic Meter in Children At-Risk for Dyslexia
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 research by Goswami and others has established that impaired rhythm and meter processing is a core deficit in developmental dyslexia, linked to atypical neural entrainment to speech. However, the structural white matter pathways that support this crucial auditory-motor integration for rhythm are poorly understood, especially in the preschool years when intervention is most effective. DTI provides a direct method to investigate the integrity of these pathways before reading onset.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that children at familial risk for dyslexia will show reduced white matter integrity, as measured by fractional anisotropy (FA), in specific auditory (e.g., acoustic radiation) and sensorimotor (e.g., superior longitudinal fasciculus) tracts, and that this reduced integrity will correlate with their neural sensitivity to rhythmic meter contrasts and phonological skills.
proposals.method: We will collect high-resolution diffusion-weighted MRI data (64 directions, b=1000 s/mm²) from 60 4- to 5-year-olds (30 at-risk, 30 controls). Using FSL's FDT toolbox, we will perform tract-based spatial statistics (TBSS) for whole-brain analysis and use probabilistic tractography (PROBTRACKX) to reconstruct specific auditory and sensorimotor pathways. DTI metrics (FA, MD) from these tracts will be correlated with pre-existing EEG measures of neural sensitivity to rhythmic meter (e.g., mismatch negativity to beat omissions) and standardized tests of phonological awareness.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first direct evidence linking the microstructural integrity of auditory-motor white matter pathways to rhythm processing deficits in pre-readers at risk for dyslexia. It will identify specific neural pathways that could serve as biomarkers for risk and targets for rhythm-based interventions aimed at improving phonological outcomes.
proposals.novelty: While prior DTI work in dyslexia has focused on reading-related tracts like the arcuate fasciculus, this proposal uniquely applies DTI to the developing auditory-sensorimotor system specifically for meter processing in a pre-reading, at-risk cohort, integrating neuroimaging with Goswami's established EEG paradigms for temporal processing.

proposals.title: Genetic architecture of auditory rhythm sensitivity and its shared heritability with developmental dyslexia: A population-level genome-wide association study
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 research by Goswami and others has established that deficits in auditory rhythm processing, particularly sensitivity to amplitude envelope rise time and meter perception, are robust behavioural and neural markers for developmental dyslexia. However, the genetic underpinnings of these foundational auditory-temporal skills and their etiological links to dyslexia remain unknown. A large-scale genetic study is needed to move beyond behavioural correlations and establish whether shared genetic risk factors explain the comorbidity between rhythm deficits and dyslexia.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesise that individual differences in meter perception and rise time sensitivity are heritable traits and that a significant proportion of their genetic variance will be shared with the genetic architecture of developmental dyslexia, implicating common biological pathways in auditory-temporal processing and literacy development.
proposals.method: We will conduct a genome-wide association study (GWAS) in a population cohort of ~10,000 children (aged 8-10) from the UK Biobank and other consortia, for whom genetic data and dyslexia diagnoses/polygenic scores are available. We will derive precise, scalable digital phenotyping measures for meter perception and rise time sensitivity using a validated tablet-based auditory processing battery. Genetic correlation (rg) and linkage disequilibrium score regression (LDSC) will be used to quantify shared heritability between the auditory traits and dyslexia. Follow-up gene-set and functional genomic analyses will identify enriched biological pathways.
proposals.contribution: This will provide the first population-level estimates of the heritability of core auditory rhythm skills in children. It will directly test the genetic overlap hypothesis for dyslexia and auditory processing deficits, offering a mechanistic, biologically-grounded account of their association. The findings could identify novel genetic targets for early risk prediction and inform the development of biologically-stratified rhythm-based interventions.
proposals.novelty: While previous genetic studies of dyslexia have focused on literacy and language phenotypes, this is the first to target its pre-linguistic auditory sensory precursors at a genetic level. It applies large-scale genomic methods, common in psychiatry, to a central but previously unexamined hypothesis in the neurodevelopmental literature on dyslexia.

proposals.title: Neural and Behavioral Sensitivity to Metrical Structure in Continuous Speech and Music: Links to Phonological Processing in Children with and without Dyslexia
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 research by Goswami and others has established that rhythmic sensitivity, particularly to beat and meter, is a core deficit in developmental dyslexia and is linked to phonological processing. However, this evidence is largely based on simplified, non-ecological stimuli like isochronous tone sequences or single words, limiting our understanding of how rhythm is processed in the complex, continuous auditory streams of real-world language and music.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that children's neural and behavioral sensitivity to meter in continuous, ecologically valid speech and music will be a stronger predictor of individual differences in phonological awareness and reading ability than sensitivity measured with simplified, isolated stimuli.
proposals.method: We will use EEG to measure neural entrainment (phase-locking) to the metrical structure of naturalistic stimuli in children aged 6-8 years (with and without dyslexia). Stimuli will include continuous children's stories and musical excerpts with clear but varying metrical patterns (e.g., 2/4 vs. 3/4 time). We will analyze the EEG data using time-frequency analysis and inter-trial phase coherence to quantify neural tracking of the meter. Behavioral tasks will involve tapping to the beat and meter identification within these continuous streams.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first direct evidence of how the brain's rhythmic processing system operates on real-world auditory input in children, clarifying the mechanistic link between rhythm and phonology. It will establish a more ecologically valid paradigm for assessing rhythmic deficits in dyslexia, with direct implications for designing interventions using continuous speech and music.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in moving beyond isolated beats to test meter perception within continuous, ecologically valid auditory streams, and in directly comparing neural entrainment to meter in both speech and music within the same child cohort at risk for dyslexia.

proposals.title: Cross-linguistic validation of a rhythm-based cognitive model of reading acquisition: Testing the role of neural sensitivity to amplitude modulation in diverse orthographies
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 research by Goswami and others has established a strong link between children's sensitivity to speech rhythm (amplitude modulation) and phonological development, which is a core deficit in dyslexia. However, the proposed 'temporal sampling' framework has been primarily tested in English, a deep orthography with complex sound-symbol mappings. It is unknown whether the model's predictive power holds across languages with different orthographic depths (e.g., shallow alphabetic, syllabic, logographic), which is crucial for developing universal and language-specific early identification and intervention strategies.
proposals.hypothesis: The strength of the relationship between neural sensitivity to amplitude modulation (AM) and phonological awareness will be moderated by orthographic depth, being strongest in deep orthographies (e.g., English) and weaker in shallow orthographies (e.g., Finnish) or non-alphabetic systems (e.g., Chinese), where other linguistic rhythms or visual-orthographic skills may play a more dominant role in reading acquisition.
proposals.method: We will recruit 6-year-old children (n=60 per group) learning to read in English (deep alphabetic), Finnish (shallow alphabetic), and Mandarin Chinese (logographic). Using EEG, we will measure neural sensitivity to within- and across-category contrasts in AM rate (2Hz vs. 4Hz), a key temporal cue for syllable and stress patterns. This neural measure will be correlated with behavioral assessments of phonological awareness, rapid automatized naming (RAN), and reading fluency. The analysis will employ hierarchical linear regression and moderation analysis to test if orthographic depth (a continuous index) significantly interacts with neural AM sensitivity in predicting reading outcomes.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first direct, cross-linguistic test of a leading neurocognitive model of reading development, moving beyond Anglo-centric evidence. It will clarify which core deficits in dyslexia are universal (e.g., basic auditory processing) versus orthography-specific, informing the design of more effective, culturally adapted literacy interventions and screening tools based on rhythm processing.
proposals.novelty: While cross-linguistic comparisons of reading exist, this is the first to empirically test the 'temporal sampling' framework's core neural mechanism—AM sensitivity—across fundamentally different writing systems, moving from correlation in one language to causal architecture validation across languages.

proposals.title: Neural Mapping of a Rhythmic Entrainment Model for Phonological Processing: An fMRI Study in Children with and without Dyslexia
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 research by Goswami and others has established a compelling cognitive model linking rhythmic entrainment, auditory temporal processing, and phonological development, with deficits in this system implicated in developmental dyslexia. However, the neural architecture supporting this model's key components—such as beat perception, temporal prediction, and syllable parsing—remains underspecified and its dysfunction in dyslexia poorly characterized at the neural systems level. Integrating neuroimaging data is crucial to move from a descriptive cognitive framework to a biologically grounded model that can inform targeted interventions.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that distinct components of the rhythmic entrainment model (e.g., low-frequency envelope tracking, delta-theta phase alignment) will map onto dissociable neural networks (e.g., auditory-motor networks involving the superior temporal gyrus, premotor cortex, and basal ganglia), and that children with dyslexia will show reduced functional connectivity within and between these networks specifically during tasks requiring precise temporal prediction for speech parsing.
proposals.method: We will use functional Magnetic Resonance Imaging (fMRI) with children (aged 7-9 years) with and without dyslexia. Participants will complete auditory tasks designed to isolate model components: 1) a beat perception task using rhythmic tone sequences, 2) a temporal prediction task with predictable vs. jittered rhythms, and 3) a syllable detection task in speech streams. Data analysis will employ a combination of General Linear Model (GLM) contrasts to identify region-specific activation and psychophysiological interaction (PPI) analyses to assess task-modulated functional connectivity between key regions of interest (e.g., auditory cortex, inferior frontal gyrus, cerebellum).
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first integrated neural map of the rhythmic entrainment model of phonological processing, directly linking its cognitive constructs to brain function. It will identify specific neural signatures of impaired temporal processing in dyslexia, moving beyond behavioral correlations to a mechanistic, brain-based account. The findings will critically inform the development of next-generation, rhythm-based interventions by pinpointing the neural systems they should target.
proposals.novelty: While prior work has linked rhythm or speech processing to broad brain regions, this study is novel in using a precise cognitive model to design fMRI tasks that fractionate and map its sub-processes, testing their integration and breakdown in dyslexia at a neural network level.

proposals.title: A Cross-Linguistic Diagnostic Framework for Developmental Dyslexia: Validating a Core Rhythm-Processing Protocol
proposals.motivation: Current dyslexia assessments are largely language-specific, focusing on phoneme-level deficits, which complicates diagnosis in multilingual contexts and obscures potential universal cognitive markers. Pre-2026 research by Goswami and others strongly implicates a domain-general auditory rhythm processing deficit as a core, potentially language-universal, risk factor. Developing a diagnostic tool based on this framework could enable earlier, more accurate identification across languages, informing timely intervention.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that sensitivity to rhythmic auditory cues, specifically the ability to detect amplitude envelope rise time and meter in non-speech stimuli, will serve as a robust, language-independent predictor of dyslexia risk in children across typologically distinct languages (e.g., English, Mandarin, Spanish).
proposals.method: We will develop a computerised assessment battery using PsychoPy, presenting non-speech rhythmic sequences (e.g., tone pips with modulated amplitude envelopes). Key tasks will measure thresholds for detecting changes in rise time (temporal modulation sensitivity) and identifying the underlying beat (meter perception). We will employ adaptive, staircase psychophysical procedures (e.g., QUEST algorithm) to derive individual sensitivity scores. These will be validated against standardised phonological and reading measures in cohorts of 6-7-year-old children (N=150 per language) with and without familial risk for dyslexia.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first validated, theoretically grounded protocol for assessing a putative universal cognitive precursor to dyslexia. It will directly test the cross-linguistic applicability of the rhythmic deficit hypothesis and move the field towards biologically grounded, language-fair diagnostic tools. The resulting open-source battery will be a significant resource for researchers and clinicians.
proposals.novelty: While rhythm deficits are linked to dyslexia, no pre-2026 work has systematically operationalised this into a standardised, psychophysically rigorous diagnostic protocol explicitly designed for cross-linguistic validation. This moves beyond correlational studies to create a practical tool based on a processing deficit framework.

proposals.title: Cross-Linguistic Dyslexia Subtyping via Supervised Machine Learning: A Focus on Temporal and Phonological Processing Profiles
proposals.motivation: Developmental dyslexia is a heterogeneous disorder, and its cognitive manifestations are influenced by the transparency of a child's orthography. Pre-2026 research, including work by Goswami, highlights core deficits in phonological and temporal (rhythm) processing, but it remains unclear how these deficits cluster into distinct, potentially language-specific subtypes. Identifying these subtypes is crucial for moving beyond a one-size-fits-all diagnostic and intervention model.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that supervised machine learning algorithms applied to cross-linguistic cognitive profiles will reveal distinct, interpretable subtypes of dyslexia, characterized by varying degrees of impairment in auditory temporal processing (e.g., rhythm sensitivity, rise time discrimination) and phonological processing (e.g., phoneme awareness, rapid naming).
proposals.method: We will collect cognitive profiles from children aged 7-9 with dyslexia and typically developing controls across languages differing in orthographic depth (e.g., English, Spanish, German). Profiles will include behavioral measures of rhythm sensitivity, amplitude modulation detection, phonemic awareness, and rapid automatized naming. We will apply and compare the performance of several pre-2026 machine learning classifiers, including Support Vector Machines (SVMs) and Random Forests, for initial binary classification (dyslexia vs. control). For subtyping, we will use unsupervised methods like Gaussian Mixture Models and hierarchical clustering on the dyslexia group data, followed by feature importance analysis from the Random Forest to interpret the cognitive dimensions defining each cluster.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide a data-driven, cross-linguistic taxonomy of dyslexia subtypes, directly linking them to pre-2026 theoretical frameworks on temporal and phonological processing. It will offer a proof-of-concept for using pattern recognition to personalize cognitive risk profiles, which can inform the future design of targeted, mechanism-based interventions tailored to a child's specific neurocognitive profile.
proposals.novelty: While machine learning has been used for single-language dyslexia classification, its application to discover *cross-linguistic subtypes* based on well-specified auditory-temporal and phonological theories is novel. This approach moves beyond correlation to identify latent population structure, integrating Goswami's temporal processing framework with modern computational analytics.

proposals.title: Cross-linguistic rhythmic and phonological patterning in child-directed speech: A corpus-based analysis of input frequency and its implications for dyslexia risk
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 research by Goswami and others establishes that children's sensitivity to speech rhythm is foundational for phonological development and literacy acquisition, and that deficits in this domain are a core marker of dyslexia risk. However, the precise rhythmic and phonological features of the linguistic input that drive this development remain poorly quantified, especially across languages. A systematic, data-driven analysis of child-directed speech (CDS) corpora is needed to model these critical input differences and inform more targeted, language-specific early interventions.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that significant cross-linguistic differences exist in the frequency and distribution of specific rhythmic units (e.g., stress feet, syllable timing) and phonological contrasts (e.g., voice onset time categories) in child-directed speech, and that these statistical properties of the input predict the developmental trajectory of phonological sensitivity in typical and at-risk learners.
proposals.method: We will apply corpus linguistics and computational phonology methods to large, existing CDS corpora (e.g., CHILDES for English, other publicly available corpora for languages like Spanish, French, and Mandarin). Using forced-alignment tools (e.g., the Montreal Forced Aligner) and custom Python scripts, we will extract and quantify the frequency of rhythmic structures (e.g., using metrical grid analysis) and phonological categories. Statistical modeling (mixed-effects regression) will compare distributions across languages and correlate them with existing behavioral data on children's phonological sensitivity and rhythm processing.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first large-scale, quantitative model of rhythmic and phonological patterning in CDS across multiple languages, directly linking input statistics to developmental outcomes. It will generate testable predictions about which specific input features are most salient for phonological learning, offering a data-driven framework for designing language-appropriate rhythm-based screening tools and intervention programs for children at risk for dyslexia.
proposals.novelty: While prior work has noted rhythmic differences between languages, this proposal novelly integrates Goswami's focus on precise temporal processing (e.g., sensitivity to VOT) with a big-data corpus approach to quantify the *actual input statistics* that shape this sensitivity, moving beyond laboratory speech to naturalistic data to explain cross-linguistic differences in dyslexia profiles.

proposals.title: A Cross-National Epidemiological Study of Developmental Dyslexia: Prevalence, Phonological Profiles, and Links to Speech Rhythm Sensitivity
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026, the prevalence of developmental dyslexia is estimated at 5-10%, but figures vary widely across studies and languages, lacking robust population-level data. Large-scale epidemiological studies are rare, and the potential role of early auditory and rhythmic processing deficits as a cross-linguistic risk factor is not well characterized at a population level. This gap hinders the development of universal screening tools and targeted, evidence-based interventions.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that (1) the population prevalence of dyslexia will show significant cross-national variation linked to linguistic orthographic depth, and (2) that deficits in speech rhythm sensitivity and categorical perception of voice onset time (VOT) will be robust, language-independent behavioural markers of dyslexia risk, detectable in large-scale screenings.
proposals.method: We will conduct a multi-national cohort study, screening 10,000+ children aged 5-7 years across three nations with contrasting orthographies (e.g., English, Spanish, Finnish). Screening will use a tablet-based battery validated for group administration, incorporating (a) a standardised reading fluency/accuracy measure, (b) a rhythmic priming task (Goswami et al., 2013) assessing sensitivity to amplitude envelope rise time, and (c) a two-alternative forced-choice VOT categorical perception task. Data will be analysed using multilevel logistic regression to model dyslexia diagnosis prevalence by nation, and receiver operating characteristic (ROC) curve analysis will evaluate the diagnostic sensitivity and specificity of the rhythm and VOT tasks.
proposals.contribution: This will provide the first directly comparable, large-scale epidemiological data on dyslexia prevalence and associated auditory-phonological profiles across orthographies. It will establish the population-level validity of rhythm processing measures as a low-cost, language-agnostic screening tool, informing public health strategies for early identification. The dataset will serve as a foundational resource for future genetic and intervention studies.
proposals.novelty: While individual labs have studied rhythm deficits in dyslexia, applying these paradigms in a harmonised, population-scale epidemiological design across nations is novel. It moves beyond small-scale, single-language case-control studies to test the universality of the auditory processing deficit hypothesis at a public health scale.

proposals.title: A Unified Computational Framework for Rhythm Processing: Modelling Typical Development and Dyslexia as Points on a Continuum of Temporal Sensitivity
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 research by Goswami and others strongly implicates impaired rhythm and temporal processing as a core deficit in developmental dyslexia, affecting phonological development. However, most studies compare groups (impaired vs. typical), treating them as categorically distinct rather than exploring a continuous developmental space. A unified computational model is needed to specify how variations in low-level auditory timing mechanisms give rise to both the normal trajectory of language acquisition and the specific breakdowns seen in dyslexia.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that individual differences in the precision of neural entrainment to amplitude modulation (AM) rates in speech (specifically ~2 Hz, the syllable rate) can be modelled within a single framework to predict both typical developmental gains in phonological sensitivity and the profile of deficits in children with dyslexia.
proposals.method: We will develop a computational model using a hierarchical temporal processing architecture, where lower layers perform auditory feature extraction (e.g., using gammatone filterbanks) and higher layers implement oscillatory filtering and phase-locking to key AM frequencies (e.g., 1-4 Hz, 4-10 Hz). The model's core parameter will be the stability of phase-locking to 2 Hz AM. We will fit this model to longitudinal behavioral data (phonological awareness, reading) and neural data (EEG measures of neural entrainment to rhythmic speech) from a cohort of children aged 4-6, including those at familial risk for dyslexia. Model fitting will use Bayesian optimization to estimate individual parameter values.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a formal, testable model linking basic auditory rhythm processing to higher-order phonological and reading development. It will move the field beyond group comparisons by offering a quantitative, parameter-based description of developmental dyslexia within a typical developmental framework. The model will generate specific predictions for intervention, identifying which temporal processing parameters are most malleable.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in using a single computational architecture to jointly model typical and impaired developmental trajectories, explicitly parameterizing the proposed causal mechanism (neural entrainment stability) rather than treating dyslexia as a separate category. This integrates Goswami's temporal sampling framework with modern computational modelling techniques.

proposals.title: Rhythmic Entrainment to Script-Specific Perceptual Units: A Cross-Linguistic Intervention for At-Risk Learners
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 research by Goswami and others establishes that rhythmic sensitivity is a core predictor of phonological skill and literacy acquisition, with deficits linked to dyslexia. However, the dominant 'global-first' visual processing model for reading may not apply universally across writing systems (e.g., logographic Chinese vs. alphabetic English). This creates a critical gap: literacy interventions based on auditory rhythm may fail if they do not align with the optimal visual perceptual unit of the script being learned.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the efficacy of a rhythm-based literacy intervention is contingent on aligning the rhythmic training regimen with the primary perceptual unit (e.g., character, syllable, phoneme) of the specific writing system, and that this script-adaptive approach will yield superior gains in decoding fluency, particularly for children at familial risk for dyslexia.
proposals.method: We will first use EEG to identify the primary perceptual unit in English and Chinese by measuring neural decoding speed (multivariate pattern analysis) for visual stimuli at different grain sizes (global shape, radical, character; whole word, letter cluster, grapheme) during brief, tachistoscopic presentation. Subsequently, we will conduct a longitudinal training study with 5-6-year-olds at familial risk for dyslexia. Children will be assigned to a rhythm-based computer game where the pacing and feedback either reinforce the script's identified primary unit (adaptive condition) or a mismatched, global strategy (control condition). Gains will be measured using standardized tests of word reading fluency and phonological awareness.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first direct evidence linking script typology to optimised, rhythm-based perceptual training, moving beyond a one-size-fits-all intervention model. It will yield a predictive framework for designing script-adaptive literacy tools and refine theoretical models of the rhythm-reading relationship by incorporating a crucial visual-perceptual dimension.
proposals.novelty: While rhythm's link to reading is established, the novel integration of script-specific visual perceptual units with auditory rhythmic entrainment for intervention is unexplored. This challenges the assumption that a universal rhythmic mechanism supports all reading acquisition equally, proposing instead a tailored, multisensory optimisation.

proposals.title: A Dynamical Systems Model of Speech Processing: Unifying Sensory and Phonological Deficits in Developmental Dyslexia
proposals.motivation: Theories of developmental dyslexia remain polarized between phonological and auditory/sensory processing deficits, yet empirical data show impairments at both levels are often correlated. This dichotomy fails to explain the heterogeneity of the disorder and the cascading effects of interventions. A systems-level framework, analogous to shifts in ecology, is needed to understand how deficits emerge from the dynamics of the speech processing network.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a single, low-level perturbation in neural dynamics—specifically, reduced resilience to neural noise or altered synaptic gain in recurrent cortical networks—can parsimoniously explain the co-occurrence of impaired sensory acuity (e.g., for voice onset time) and phonological instability observed in children at risk for dyslexia.
proposals.method: We will develop a computational model using a reservoir computing architecture, known for its dynamical systems properties, trained on a speech processing task (e.g., categorizing acoustic continua like VOT). The model's 'health' will be parametrically degraded by manipulating internal noise levels and connection gains. We will then test whether these manipulations simultaneously produce behavioral signatures of both poor sensory discrimination and unstable phonological category formation, mirroring pre-2026 behavioral and neural data from at-risk children.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a unifying computational account that bridges the phonological and sensory deficit theories, moving the field beyond modular explanations. It will generate testable predictions for neuroimaging (e.g., altered neural oscillatory dynamics) and offer a principled rationale for why rhythm-based interventions, which may stabilize neural dynamics, can improve both phonological and auditory skills.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in applying a dynamical systems framework from ecology to speech processing deficits, explicitly modeling the deficit as a system-level property rather than a locus-specific impairment. This moves beyond simply acknowledging interactions to formally demonstrating how a single cause generates the observed profile of correlated deficits.

proposals.title: State-Dependent Oscillatory Coupling: Perceptual Bias Gates the Phonological-Attentional Deficit in Developmental Dyslexia
proposals.motivation: Current theories of developmental dyslexia often posit static deficits in phonological or auditory temporal processing. However, they struggle to explain the significant intra-individual variability in symptom presentation across different tasks and contexts. A dynamic, state-dependent framework is needed to reconcile these inconsistencies and inform more personalized intervention strategies.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the core deficit in dyslexia involves unstable phase-amplitude coupling between neural oscillations supporting auditory sampling (e.g., theta/gamma) and those supporting attentional control (e.g., alpha). Crucially, an individual's momentary perceptual bias (global vs. local) will act as a gating mechanism, determining whether the phonological or attentional component of this dysfunctional coupling is behaviorally dominant.
proposals.method: We will test 6-8-year-old children with and without dyslexia using a dual-task paradigm combining a rhythmic tone discrimination task (probing auditory sampling) with a visual global/local Navon task (manipulating perceptual bias). Concurrent high-density EEG will be recorded. We will analyze time-frequency representations and compute phase-amplitude coupling (PAC) between theta (4-8 Hz) phase and gamma (30-50 Hz) amplitude in auditory cortex, and its modulation by frontal theta-alpha (8-12 Hz) power. Cross-trial and cross-condition analyses will assess how neural coupling and behavioral performance shift with induced perceptual state.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first direct neural evidence for a state-dependent, interactive model of the dyslexia deficit, moving beyond static trait explanations. It will identify a novel biomarker—the malleability of oscillatory coupling—that predicts symptom variability and could serve as a target for rhythm-based interventions aimed at stabilizing perceptual-cognitive interactions.
proposals.novelty: This proposal uniquely integrates Goswami's temporal sampling framework with the cognitive neuroscience of perceptual bias, proposing that perceptual style is not a fixed trait but a dynamic state that gates the expression of a core oscillatory dysfunction.

proposals.title: Genetic Perceptual Style as a Moderator of GVVP Strategy Efficacy in Adolescent Foreign Language Vocabulary Learning
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 research shows that perceptual style (global/local bias) is heritable and linked to cognitive processing, including language. However, its role in moderating the effectiveness of specific, multi-sensory learning strategies like GVVP (Global-to-Visual-to-Verbal-to-Procedural) is unknown. Personalizing pedagogy based on cognitive-neurogenetic profiles could significantly improve foreign language acquisition outcomes, moving beyond one-size-fits-all approaches.
proposals.hypothesis: The efficacy of the GVVP vocabulary learning strategy will be moderated by an individual's heritable global/local perceptual style, such that learners with a genetic predisposition for a global perceptual bias will show greater learning gains from the strategy than those with a local bias.
proposals.method: We will recruit 120 typically developing adolescent-parent dyads. Perceptual style will be assessed using a validated Navon task (global/local letter identification), with heritability estimated via intra-class correlations. Genetic correlates will be explored via candidate gene analysis (e.g., dopamine-related genes like DRD4, COMT) based on pre-2026 literature linking them to perceptual attention. Participants will complete a controlled foreign language (e.g., novel pseudo-word) learning task implementing the GVVP strategy. Learning gains will be measured via pre/post accuracy and reaction time. A moderated regression analysis will test the interaction between perceptual style (and its genetic proxies) and learning condition on vocabulary acquisition.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first evidence linking heritable perceptual biases to the efficacy of a specific language learning strategy. It will contribute a novel predictive model for personalizing foreign language instruction. Furthermore, it extends Goswami's work on rhythmic and temporal processing in dyslexia to a non-dyslexic population, exploring genetic foundations of perceptual style relevant for typical learning.
proposals.novelty: It synergistically combines genetic family design, perceptual phenotyping, and a structured pedagogical intervention (GVVP) to predict learning outcomes, a fusion not attempted in pre-2026 research. It tests a direct gene-cognition-pedagogy pathway in typical adolescent language acquisition.

proposals.title: Disentangling Perception from Bias: A Signal Detection Theory Approach to Visual Crowding in Developmental Dyslexia
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 research on dyslexia has established links between phonological deficits and impaired auditory rhythm processing. However, the role of visual processing, particularly visual crowding, remains contentious, with debates over whether performance deficits reflect true perceptual impairment or differences in response strategy. A rigorous, bias-free measure of crowding is needed to clarify its specific contribution to the dyslexic phenotype and its potential interaction with auditory-temporal deficits.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that children with developmental dyslexia will show a reduced perceptual sensitivity index (d') in visual crowding tasks compared to typically developing controls, even when accounting for response criterion, and that this visual d' will correlate with established measures of auditory temporal (e.g., rise time) and phonological sensitivity.
proposals.method: We adapt the classic Signal Detection Theory (SDT) framework from psychoacoustics to a visual crowding paradigm. Children (aged 8-10 years, with and without dyslexia) will complete a target identification task (e.g., a central 'T' or 'L') flanked by distractors, with target-flanker spacing and contrast systematically varied. For each condition, we will record hit rates (correct target ID) and false alarm rates (incorrect ID on catch trials with no target). Perceptual sensitivity (d') and response criterion (c) will be computed separately using standard SDT formulas, providing a purer measure of crowding strength than accuracy alone.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first direct, bias-free quantification of visual crowding sensitivity in dyslexia using a well-established psychophysical model. It will clarify the nature of the visual deficit and test for cross-modal correlations between visual d' and auditory temporal processing metrics, offering a novel, integrated perspective on dyslexic processing across sensory domains.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the methodological cross-pollination: applying the rigorous SDT framework, standard in audiology for isolating sensitivity, to the unresolved visual crowding debate in dyslexia. This allows a direct test of whether crowding is a genuine perceptual bottleneck or a strategic difference, a distinction previous accuracy-based studies could not make.

proposals.title: Right-Hemisphere Network Dysconnectivity in Developmental Dyslexia: A Graph-Theoretic Analysis of Rhythmic and Prosodic Processing
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 research, including work by Goswami, highlights impaired rhythmic and prosodic sensitivity as a core deficit in developmental dyslexia, functions strongly linked to right-hemisphere networks. However, the specific organization and integrity of these right-hemisphere networks in children with dyslexia remain poorly characterized. Applying network neuroscience can move beyond regional activation to understand how dysconnectivity within these circuits underpins the phonological and temporal processing deficits central to the disorder.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that children with developmental dyslexia will exhibit altered topological properties (e.g., reduced efficiency, modularity) in right-hemisphere fronto-temporal and cerebellar networks involved in acoustic rhythm processing, and that the severity of this dysconnectivity will correlate with behavioral measures of prosodic sensitivity and phonological awareness.
proposals.method: We will collect resting-state and auditory rhythm task-based fMRI from 60 children (8-10 years), half with dyslexia. Using CONN toolbox and SPM12, we will construct functional connectivity matrices for a right-hemisphere mask encompassing superior temporal gyrus, inferior frontal gyrus, and cerebellum. Graph-theoretic analysis (using the Brain Connectivity Toolbox) will compute network metrics (global/local efficiency, clustering coefficient, betweenness centrality).
